title: WordPiece and SentencePiece
concept: WordPiece merges pairs that maximize likelihood; SentencePiece treats spaces as standard characters, eliminating language-specific word-splitting rules.

exercises.prompt: What is the main goal of text tokenization in AI? tokenization in AI?ization of a text tokenizer in machine learning tokenizers?

options.text: Chopping text into smaller pieces called tokens.
Translating sentences directly into human speech.
Checking the text for spelling and grammar errors.

exercises.explain: Tokenization is the crucial first step where raw text is chopped up into smaller units (tokens) that an AI model can process and understand.

cards.title: The Space Mystery
cards.body: When computers read text, they break sentences into pieces. But how do they handle languages like Chinese or Japanese that don't use spaces between words? Let's explore the clever tricks AIs use to chop up text without getting lost in translation!

cards.title: What are WordPiece and SentencePiece?
cards.body: WordPiece and SentencePiece are subword tokenizers. WordPiece splits words into smaller units by choosing merges that make the training data most predictable. SentencePiece takes this further by treating spaces just like regular letters, meaning it doesn't need pre-split words to start working.

cards.title: The Paper-Cutting Analogy
cards.body: Think of standard splitting like cutting paper only along dotted lines. If a language doesn't have spaces (dotted lines), you get stuck! SentencePiece is like a pair of scissors that doesn't care about dotted lines; it treats spaces like normal text characters, cutting wherever it makes the most sense.

cards.title: Tokenization in Action
cards.body: In WordPiece, the word "playful" might be split into "play" and "##ful" (the "##" means it connects to the previous piece). In SentencePiece, a sentence like "I play" is tokenized as ["_I", "_play"], where the space is preserved as a visible character "_" instead of being thrown away.

cards.title: Myth: AIs Must Read Whole Words
cards.body: Many believe that computers must find whole words before they can understand a sentence. In reality, modern models use subword pieces! This prevents them from getting stuck on brand-new or misspelled words, as they can just look at the smaller familiar parts.

cards.title: Space Handling Comparison
cards.body: Let's compare how WordPiece and SentencePiece handle spaces and word boundaries in text preprocessing.

left.text: Requires text to be split by spaces first. Replaces inner spaces with special joining markers like '##'.

right.text: Treats spaces as actual characters (e.g., '_'). Does not need to pre-split text by spaces.

exercises.prompt: How does the WordPiece algorithm decide which subword units to merge together?

options.text: It randomly selects pairs to merge.
It merges the pair that maximizes the likelihood of the training data.
It splits words at every single letter.

exercises.explain: WordPiece evaluates potential symbol pairs and chooses the one that increases the likelihood of the training data the most, rather than just choosing the most frequent pair.

exercises.prompt: True or False: SentencePiece is lossless, meaning you can perfectly reconstruct the original sentence from the generated tokens.
exercises.explain: This is true! Because SentencePiece treats spaces as regular characters, it can reconstruct the exact original sentence (including spaces) from the tokens without losing information.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the sentence about SentencePiece.
exercises.sentence: SentencePiece is unique because it treats __blank__ as regular characters.
exercises.explain: SentencePiece treats spaces as normal characters (often represented by a special symbol like '_'), allowing it to tokenize text without needing language-specific rules to split words first.

exercises.prompt: Identify the statement about tokenizers that contains a factual error (the AI hallucination):

options.text: SentencePiece can easily handle languages like Japanese because it doesn't rely on space characters to find word boundaries.
WordPiece builds its vocabulary by choosing pieces that make the training text highly predictable.
WordPiece treats spaces as standard characters, which is why it doesn't need pre-tokenization rules.

exercises.explain: Sentence C is AI-generated because WordPiece relies on pre-tokenization (splitting by spaces first) and does not treat spaces as standard characters. Sentence A and B are correct descriptions of how these tokenizers function.

exercises.prompt: Imagine you are building a translation AI that needs to read both English and Japanese. Why would you choose SentencePiece over WordPiece for this multilingual task?

options.text: SentencePiece treats spaces as normal characters, while WordPiece merges pairs based on likelihood after spaces split the text.
WordPiece only works on English, while SentencePiece is used exclusively for coding languages.
SentencePiece requires pre-tokenization with spaces, while WordPiece does not use space characters at all.
Both models discard all space characters and only look at vowel patterns to split words.

exercises.explain: SentencePiece treats space characters as standard symbols (often using a special underscore character) and doesn't require pre-splitting text. WordPiece, on the other hand, requires text to be split into words by spaces first, and then it merges the fragments that maximize the statistical likelihood of the training data.
